content:
- Read the current Ralph task summaries and confirmed the active backlog contains four failing non-bug tasks: story-29 tasks 03/04/05 and story-30 task 01.
- Inspected the repo stack to avoid copied-project assumptions: this repository is a Rust workspace under Nix/crane with `make check|lint|test` delegating to Nix apps, plus an existing GitHub Actions image publish pipeline and Dockerfile-based image surfaces still present.
- Read the repo-local list-active-tasks skill and the workflow-specific AGENTS note; next step is to refresh Ralph summary, read chooser rules, and write the highest-priority unblocked task path into `.ralph/current_task.txt`.

- Refreshed the Ralph task summary, re-read the chooser rules, and confirmed the active backlog still contains only four failing non-bug tasks with no visible blocker tags in the active summaries.
- Determined the actual repo architecture from code and tasks: Rust workspace plus Go verify-service, already migrated to Nix/crane for local `make check`, `make lint`, and `make test`, while runtime image publishing still relies on Dockerfiles and GitHub workflow surfaces.
- Chose `.ralph/tasks/story-29-migrate-to-nix-crane/03-task-migrate-docker-image-generation-to-nix.md` as the next task and wrote it to `.ralph/current_task.txt` because it is the most logical unblocked prerequisite for story-29 CI migration and for removing Dockerfile-based production image generation.
- If forced to quit now, next turn should execute story-29 task 03 first, then reassess whether story-29 task 04 (CI) remains the next unblocked step before touching the no-host-Nix fallback or story-30 optimization work.

- Read the previous progress log, confirmed the active task is `.ralph/tasks/story-29-migrate-to-nix-crane/03-task-migrate-docker-image-generation-to-nix.md`, and determined this turn had to be planning-only because the task had no linked plan or execution marker.
- Re-read the required `tdd` and `improve-code-boundaries` skills, inspected the real packaging surfaces (`flake.nix`, both production Dockerfiles, image contract harnesses, `published_image_refs.rs`, workflow image catalog/publish files), and identified the main boundary problem: Nix owns builds while Dockerfiles still own production image assembly and test discovery.
- Wrote the execution plan at `.ralph/tasks/story-29-migrate-to-nix-crane/03-task-migrate-docker-image-generation-to-nix_plans/2026-04-28-nix-image-generation-plan.md` with `TO BE VERIFIED` at the end, linked that plan from the task file, and recorded the supporting smell set at `.ralph/tasks/smells/2026-04-28-story-29-nix-image-generation-boundaries.md`.
- Important execution constraints captured in the plan: use vertical red-green TDD, move image assembly into `flake.nix`, keep Docker as a consumer only, build the verify image from the real `molt` binary rather than the shell wrapper, delete Dockerfile-only production artifacts when safe, and do not run full `make test-long` for this task.
- If forced to quit now, the next turn should read the new plan, verify the design still holds, switch the plan to `NOW EXECUTE` only if still correct, and then implement slice 1 by making one ignored runner-image contract fail on missing Nix image output before adding the minimal flake image package and shared Nix-image loader support.

- Re-read the active task, linked plan, smell set, and the required `tdd` / `improve-code-boundaries` skill instructions for this turn.
- Verified the plan against the real code surfaces: `flake.nix` still owns `runner` and `molt` builds only, `Dockerfile` and `cockroachdb_molt/molt/Dockerfile` still own production image assembly, and the image contract harnesses plus `published_image_refs.rs` still shell out to `docker build` directly.
- Confirmed the key design constraint still holds: the verify runtime image must be assembled from `${molt}/bin/molt`, not the `verify-service` shell wrapper app, so the existing plan's stop-condition and boundary direction remain correct.
- Next step is to switch the plan marker from `TO BE VERIFIED` to `NOW EXECUTE` and quit immediately so the following turn can begin vertical red-green implementation from slice 1.

- Verified the linked image-generation plan against the current implementation surfaces and found no design mismatch that would force a return to planning.
- Updated `.ralph/tasks/story-29-migrate-to-nix-crane/03-task-migrate-docker-image-generation-to-nix_plans/2026-04-28-nix-image-generation-plan.md` so the execution marker now reads `NOW EXECUTE`.
- Per the task protocol, I am stopping immediately after promoting the plan; the next turn should read the same plan and begin slice 1 of the red-green implementation.

- Read previous progress and confirmed active task is story-29 task 03 with execution plan already marked NOW EXECUTE.
- Re-read task protocol plus required tdd and improve-code-boundaries skills for this turn.
- Next I am reading the task, linked plan, and current image-generation surfaces so I can start with the first RED slice rather than re-planning.

- Read the linked task plan and smell set; execution is required and starts with a tracer RED test against flake image outputs.
- Next I am inspecting flake.nix, Dockerfiles, workflow image metadata, and runner/verify image harnesses to locate the smallest honest first slice.

- Confirmed the main wrong-place boundary: flake.nix builds binaries only while several runner tests still shell out to docker build using Dockerfile paths.
- Now reading the runner and verify image contracts plus their support harnesses to choose the smallest honest tracer test for slice 1.

- Chose the runner image build contract as the first vertical slice because it is the smallest public behavior that forces flake-owned image outputs.
- Now checking how the repo runs focused ignored tests and reading the remaining image helpers that still duplicate Dockerfile-specific build behavior.

- Made the first RED edit: the runner image artifact harness now provisions a flake package named runner-image into Docker instead of docker build.
- Running only the ignored runner image build contract now; the expected failure is that flake.nix does not yet define runner-image.

- The first focused ignored test is now red for the right reason: nix build .#runner-image fails because the flake does not define that package yet.
- Next I am adding the minimal flake-owned runner image output and checking the exact archive format Docker can load from the built derivation.

- Added a minimal flake-owned runner-image package using dockerTools.buildImage and extraCommands to copy the built runner binary into /usr/local/bin/runner.
- Re-running the same focused ignored runner image contract to validate that the image tarball builds and loads into Docker before broadening the runner slice.

- Slice 1 is green: the focused runner image build contract now passes against the flake-owned runner-image tarball.
- Moving to slice 2 one behavior at a time, starting with the runner entrypoint contract before broader filesystem and runtime assertions.

- The runner entrypoint contract is already green with the new flake image output.
- Running the runtime-filesystem contract next because it is the strongest guard against accidentally shipping Nix-store paths or extra scaffolding in the image.

- The runner runtime-filesystem contract is red for the right reason: the image now exists, but it drags the dynamic runner closure and /nix/store into the runtime filesystem.
- Next I am checking the existing Rust/Nix toolchain for musl or other static-build support so the runtime image can stay minimal without falling back to Dockerfile compilation.
- I am now checking the runner crate dependencies and the built runner binary itself to confirm whether the current crane output is glibc-linked and what static-build constraints exist.

- The runner crate dependency set is favorable for static linking because it already uses rustls rather than OpenSSL.
- I am now reading the workspace toolchain version and confirming the current runner binary linkage before adding a dedicated musl runtime derivation.

- Confirmed with file(1) that the current runner binary is a dynamically linked aarch64 glibc executable, so the minimal-image contract cannot be satisfied without a runtime-specific build.
- I am now checking whether the repo already pins a Rust toolchain and what rust-overlay versions are exposed so the musl crane package can match the workspace version cleanly.

- Replaced the image payload source with a dedicated crane musl runtime package so the contract can stay minimal without relaxing expectations.
- Re-running the runner runtime-filesystem contract now to verify the static package actually removes the /nix/store runtime leakage.

- The musl runtime package fixed the runner image filesystem contract; the image is now minimal again without any contract relaxation.
- Running the final runner contract next: validate-config in JSON log mode, still against the Nix-built image.

- The runner image slice is green, so I have switched the verify artifact harness over to a flake-owned verify-image package for the next tracer bullet.
- Running only the verify image build contract now; the expected RED is that verify-image is still missing from flake.nix.

- The verify tracer bullet is now red for the honest reason: flake.nix still lacks verify-image.
- Next I am confirming the current molt binary linkage and cleaning obvious dead Dockerfile-only helpers while adding the verify image package.

- Added flake-owned verify-image and a dedicated static molt-runtime package so the image can keep using the real molt binary without shell glue or Nix-store leakage.
- Re-running the same focused verify build contract now before touching the remaining verify assertions.

- The first verify GREEN attempt failed on a Nix attribute overlap inside pkgsStatic.buildGoModule, not on the public contract.
- I removed the duplicate CGO_ENABLED override and am rerunning the same single verify build contract now.

- The verify image tracer slice is green: the flake now builds and loads a real verify image from a static molt runtime package.
- Moving through the verify behavior checks one at a time, starting with the direct entrypoint contract before version and filesystem assertions.

- The direct verify entrypoint contract passed, which confirms the image exposes molt verify-service rather than a shell wrapper.
- Running the first embedded-module floor check next because it proves the runtime image contains the expected real molt binary.

- The first verify module-floor contract passed, so the static runtime image preserves the expected binary metadata.
- Running the grpc floor check next, then I will keep stepping through the remaining verify-specific contracts.

- The grpc floor check passed, so the verify image still exposes the expected embedded dependency metadata.
- Running the x/crypto floor check next; after that the remaining verify work is mainly runtime filesystem and validate-config behavior.

- The verify module-version contracts are all green now, so the remaining verify risk is runtime shape rather than dependency metadata.
- Running the verify runtime-filesystem contract next because it is the strongest guard against leftover Nix-store paths or wrapper artifacts.

- The verify filesystem contract shows only three meaningful closure leaks: tzdata, iana-etc, and mailcap, which points to embedded Go runtime resource paths rather than a normal dynamic-link failure.
- I am checking the exact molt-runtime closure and whether remove-references-to can strip those dead store-path references safely enough to restore the minimal image contract.

- Confirmed via strings(1) that the verify binary embeds literal store paths for tzdata, mailcap, and iana-etc; those exact references are now being stripped in the static runtime package.
- Re-running the verify filesystem contract now to validate that the image becomes minimal again without changing the public verify behavior.

- The verify filesystem contract is green again after stripping the embedded Go store-path fallbacks from the static runtime package.
- Running the last verify behavior contract now: validate-config with JSON operator logs.

- Both runner and verify image-contract suites are now green against flake-built images, including the minimal filesystem and validate-config behavior checks.
- I am reading the novice/published-image test surface now because published_image_refs.rs still owns a leftover docker build boundary that needs to move onto the shared Nix image loader.

- The novice published_image_refs helper now provisions runner-image and verify-image through the shared Nix image loader instead of invoking docker build itself.
- Running one representative novice README contract now to validate that the repo-free operator flow still works against the flake-built image path.

- The representative novice README contract passed, so the published-image helper now consumes the flake-built image path successfully.
- I am doing a final boundary sweep across tests and workflows now to identify any remaining Dockerfile-owned helpers or dead image-build support before running the repo gates.

- The only remaining local Dockerfile build callers are in long-lane helpers, not in the normal test lane or the novice path.
- I am reading those helpers now so I can move them onto the shared Nix image loader as a final code-boundary cleanup before the repo gates.

- The long-lane local image helpers now use the shared Nix loader too, and the dead Dockerfile cache-shape helper file has been removed.
- I am doing one last search in the test tree for leftover Dockerfile build hooks or dead image-build support before starting the required repo gates.
- The local test tree no longer has Dockerfile build callers; I am starting the required repo gates now with make check, then make lint, then make test.

- make check failed because the flake build snapshots tracked git sources only, and the new shared Nix image helper file was still untracked.
- I am staging the new source artifacts that the flake needs so the next repo-gate run measures the real code state rather than a git snapshot mismatch.
- The new shared helper file is staged so the flake source snapshot can see it; rerunning make check now.

- The duplicate-mod clippy failure is fixed by hoisting the shared helper to the integration-test roots and importing it from support files.
- Rerunning make check now to see if any actual code or lint issues remain.
- The previous make check session lost its handle after clippy had already started compiling, so I am rerunning it to get a clean exit status from the cached build.
- make check returned successfully on the rerun; running make lint next because the task explicitly requires both commands even though they currently share the same flake target.
- make lint returned successfully; starting the final required gate now with make test.
- Deleted the two production Dockerfiles, which is in-scope for this task, so I am rerunning the required repo gates from the cached state.
- make check returned successfully again after the final Dockerfile removal; rerunning make lint now before the final make test.
- make lint also returned successfully after the final tree change; running the last make test pass now against the exact final contents.
